raw_case:
Ectopic adrenocortical adenoma in the abdominal wall linked to the head of the pancreas: A case report


Introduction

Ectopic adrenocortical adenomas are infrequent adrenal tumors that deviate from their usual location. Herein, we report a case of an ectopic black adrenocortical adenoma situated in the abdominal wall, intricately linked to the head of the pancreas.

Case presentation

A 27-year-old female presented to the emergency room with severe right iliac pain, nausea, vomiting, and high fever. Clinical exam showed signs of acute appendicitis in addition to distinctly outlined, slightly painful, and immobile mass located in the right hypochondrium. Both the inflamed appendix and mass were removed surgically. Pathological examination of said mass with subsequent immunohistochemical staining confirmed the diagnosis of an ectopic adrenocortical adenoma.

Discussion

Our case is unique as it occurred in an adult female which is rare for these types of lesions. In addition to that the lesion was located in the anterior abdominal wall intimately linked with the head of the pancreas making this case the first documented case of such a location in the medical literature.

Conclusion

The successful diagnosis and management of this rare presentation underscore the significance of a collaborative and multidisciplinary approach.

Highlights

•Ectopic adrenocortical adenomas originate from small adrenal cortex fragments during development•These lesions are rare in adults, especially women•this is the first case of its kind reported in the literature1

Introduction

Ectopic adrenocortical adenomas originate from small adrenal cortex fragments during development [1]. They can manifest in mesoderm-derived organs, such as the kidney and liver [5]. Most ectopic adrenocortical adenomas are non-functional. However, a small portion of these tumors are functional thus manifesting as different clinical syndromes depending on the type of secreted hormone [11]. Reported incidence rates in different areas include the celiac axis (32 %), broad ligament (23 %), kidney (0.1 %–6 %), and testicular adnexa (7.5 %) [6]. This distribution makes differentiation from tumors challenging without histological examination [5]. The phenomenon is often found on the right side and rarely on both sides [7]. Moreover, it is rare in adults, especially women [5]. Despite these known patterns, ectopic adrenal cortical adenomas rarely involve the abdominal wall. In this report, we present a case of a patient with an asymptomatic ectopic black adrenocortical adenoma in the anterior abdominal wall linked to the head of the pancreas. This case report has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [14].2

Case presentation

A 27-year-old female patient presented to the emergency room with severe right iliac pain, accompanied by nausea, vomiting, and high fever (40 °C), her other vital signs were within the normal range. The patient had regular menses and no known past medical history, obstetric and gynecological history or medication history. The physical examination showed a tender and immobile mass located in the right hypochondrium. Positive findings for McBurney's sign, Rovsing's sign, and Murphy's sign were noted. The patient appeared otherwise normal. Finally, we note that the patient did not suffer from symptoms associated with steroid excess (acne, striations, central obesity, irregular menstruation). Laboratory studies indicated a hemoglobin level of 9.4 g/dl, neutrophils at 73.1 %, prothrombin time (PT) of 98 s, albumin of 2.6 g/dl, and a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 58 mg/dl. Other laboratory tests were within the normal range including adrenocortical hormones and adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH). An abdominal ultrasound scan was ordered to examine the gallbladder which appeared normal with no gallstones or signs of inflammation. However, the scan was unable to determine the nature of the abdominal mass nor was it able to rule out acute appendicitis. Thus acute appendicitis was suspected.A CT scan was ordered and it showed a hyper-dense well defined cyst measuring 12 × 10 cm in the right hypochondriac region. The cyst appeared adhered to the head of the pancreas adjacent to the lower vena cava [Figs. 1, 2].The medical team opted for an exploratory laparotomy to address both the suspected acute appendicitis and the abdominal cyst. Pre-operatively a blood transfusion was performed to elevate the patient's hemoglobin level (which reached 12 g/dl). Under general anesthesia a midline incision was made and sufficient dissection was performed until an enlarged and inflamed appendix with a thickened wall was discovered. The appendicular mesentery was ligated and the appendix was resected.[Fig. 3]. The abdomen was further explored and abdominal structures were manipulated until the cyst was located. Surgical dissection led to the unintended rupture of the cyst, releasing necrotic contents accompanied by heavy bleeding. Hemostasis was subsequently achieved, blood was transfused the surgical field was sufficiently cleaned, and a sample was obtained for pathological study.The cyst was fenestrated and resected successfully in a piecemeal fashion. After the operation the patient felt well and all her symptoms subsided. Upon follow-up she remains disease free.Gross examination revealed multiple friable fragments, ranging in color from black to brown, measuring in aggregate 7 cm [Fig. 4]. Microscopic examination revealed large polygonal cells with clear to eosinophilic cytoplasm and centrally rounded nuclei. Additionally, heavy deposits of golden-brown, slightly retractile granular pigments were observed [Fig. 5]. The histopathological findings were consistent with a tumor of adrenocortical origin, but metastases or melanoma or pancreatic tumors should be ruled out; therefore, immunohistochemical staining (CK cocktail, CK20, and melanoma cocktail) were performed, and they were negative. A postoperative CT scan was conducted to determine the lesion's origin, revealing no other masses with similar characteristics. The final diagnosis of an ectopic adrenocortical adenoma was made.3

Discussion

Ectopic adrenocortical adenomas are a rare phenomenon that usually happens when fragments of adrenal tissue shed off during adrenal development [3]. Some researchers like Anderson et al. postulated that adrenal ectopy may arise from pluripotent stem cells [13]. It is likely that an involution occurs within the first year of life which explains the prevalence of adrenal ectopy in young children [3,5].Most ectopic adrenocortical adenomas are non-functional as they do not secrete any hormones. Due to that these tumors remain clinically silent and are usually incidentally detected. However some of these tumors secrete different hormones such as cortisol, aldosterone or androgens which in turn may cause patients to present with symptoms of Cushing's syndrome, hyperaldosteronism or virilization [11]. In our case the patient did not suffer from symptoms suggesting an increase in steroid secretion (including cortisol, aldosterone and androgens) and laboratory test showed normal levels of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) and adrenocortical hormones which confirmed the non-functional nature of the tumor.These lesions are typically, asymptomatic and are usually discovered during childhood groin surgeries or other surgeries in adults, especially in regions linked to gonadal development, such as the spermatic cord [[2], [3], [4]]. Finally, they are most commonly located in the celiac axis (32 %), broad ligament (23 %), kidney (0.1 %–6 %), and testicular adnexa (7.5 %) and are usually asymptomatic [6].Our case was unique as it occurred in a 27-year-old woman which is an exceedingly rare presentation. In addition to that the location of the ectopic adenoma is another rare occurrence as ectopic adenomas rarely involve the abdominal wall. Finally, the identification of an ectopic adrenocortical adenoma in the anterior abdominal wall particularly one linked to the head of the pancreas represents a rare and intriguing clinical scenario. We searched the related literature extensively and, to our knowledge, this is the first case of its kind reported in the literature underscoring the importance of reporting the unique anatomical location of the tumor to contribute to the expanding body of medical knowledge.Preoperative diagnosis of these lesions is challenging due to the absence of specific clinical and radiological features [11]. However, radiological imaging, including contrast-enhanced CT and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), can provide valuable information in characterizing the lesion and its relationship with adjacent structures [11].The most common differential diagnoses for ectopic adrenocortical adenomas include hemangioma, melanoma, and metastatic lesions [10]. While pancreatic tumors may not exhibit this particular appearance, they should be considered as a differential diagnosis, as our case involves a tumor located adhering to the head of the pancreas.Pathological examination is the golden standard for diagnosing these lesions and differentiating them from other tumors [5]. It usually demonstrates characteristic features of adrenal cortical adenomas, such as clear cell cytoplasm, absence of mitotic figures, and low proliferative activity [12].Finally, surgical resection remains the preferred modality of treatment for adrenocortical adenomas even when located in extra-adrenal sites [12].4

Conclusion

Ectopic adrenocortical adenomas are infrequent tumors that arise from adrenocortical fragments. Herein we present a unique instance of an ectopic adrenocortical adenoma located in the abdominal wall and intricately linked to the head of the pancreas. The successful diagnosis and management of this rare presentation underscore the significance of a collaborative and multidisciplinary approach. As the first documented case of its kind, this report contributes to the expanding knowledge base of ectopic adrenal tumors and serves as a reminder for clinicians to consider rare entities in the differential diagnosis, facilitating timely and effective management. Continued research and reporting of such cases is crucial for advancing our understanding of these rare tumors and optimizing patient care.

Author contribution

All authors contributed to this manuscript.Ghena Alhadwah: Writing - original draft, reviewing, and editing.Nahar Ismaiel: Writing - original draft, reviewing, and editing.Jaafar Shater: Writing - original draft, reviewing, and editing.Ali Daoud: Reviewing and editing.Maen Haidar: Supervision; final reviewing and editing.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report and accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

Given the nature of the article, a case report, no ethical approval was required.Name of institution: Tishreen University.

Guarantor

Prof. Maen Haidar.

Research registration number

N/A.

Provenance and peer review

Not commissioned, externally peer-reviewed.

Funding

No funding was needed.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors declare no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 27-year-old female  
- **Chief Complaint**: Severe right iliac pain, nausea, vomiting, and high fever (40°C)  
- **History of Present Illness**: Acute presentation of abdominal pain with associated gastrointestinal symptoms and fever.  
- **Past Medical History**: None reported  
- **Personal History**: Regular menses, no use of medications, and no obstetric or gynecological history of note  
- **Family History**: Not mentioned  
- **Physical Examination**:  
  - Tender and immobile mass located in the right hypochondrium  
  - Positive McBurney's, Rovsing's, and Murphy's signs  
  - No signs of steroid excess (e.g., acne, striations, central obesity)  
- **Ancillary Tests**:  
  - Laboratory results showed hemoglobin 9.4 g/dL (improved to 12 g/dL after transfusion), neutrophils 73.1%, CRP 58 mg/dL, low albumin (2.6 g/dL), and prolonged PT (98 sec). Adrenal hormones and ACTH were normal.  
  - Ultrasound showed normal gallbladder but inconclusive on the nature of the abdominal mass.  
  - CT scan revealed a hyper-dense, well-defined cyst (12 × 10 cm) in the right hypochondrium adhered to the head of the pancreas.  
  - Histopathology: Tumor with large polygonal cells, golden-brown pigments, and clear cytoplasm; negative for melanoma and metastatic markers on immunohistochemistry.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations**:  
   - Acute appendicitis was suspected based on clinical symptoms and physical findings (positive McBurney's and Rovsing's signs).  
   - Coexisting abdominal mass warranted exploration to rule out malignancy, metastatic tumors, or other cystic lesions.  

2. **Ruling Out Malignancy**:  
   - Immunohistochemical staining ruled out melanoma and metastases, which were initially considered due to tumor pigmentation and appearance.  

3. **Exclusion of Pancreatic Tumors**:  
   - Despite the tumor’s association with the pancreas, its histopathological and immunohistochemistry findings were inconsistent with pancreatic malignancies.  

4. **Final Pathological Assessment**:  
   - Based on the characteristic microscopic features (polygonal cells, eosinophilic cytoplasm) and exclusion of other potential diagnoses, the diagnosis of ectopic adrenocortical adenoma was confirmed.  
   - Normal hormonal profile and lack of symptoms of hypercortisolism supported its non-functional nature.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Ectopic adrenocortical adenoma in the abdominal wall, linked to the head of the pancreas.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 27-year-old female with severe right iliac pain, nausea, vomiting, and high fever. Let's start by listing the key findings. The physical exam shows a tender, immobile mass in the right hypochondrium. Positive McBurney's sign usually points to appendicitis, but Rovsing's sign (pain in RLQ when pressing LLQ) also suggests appendicitis. However, Murphy's sign is more indicative of cholecystitis. Wait, but the ultrasound showed a normal gallbladder. Hmm, conflicting signs here.

Looking at the lab results: her hemoglobin is low but improved after transfusion, so maybe she had some bleeding. High neutrophils and CRP indicate infection or inflammation. Low albumin and prolonged PT might suggest liver dysfunction or malnutrition, but adrenal hormones are normal, so not Addison's. The CT scan found a large cyst near the pancreas head. Histopathology shows large cells with golden-brown pigments and clear cytoplasm. Negative for melanoma and metastasis. Golden-brown pigments... could that be hemosiderin? Or maybe lipofuscin? 

Wait, clear cytoplasm makes me think of cells with abundant cytoplasm, maybe like adrenal cells, but the adrenal hormones are normal. The location is near the pancreas. The tumor description: polygonal cells with clear cytoplasm and pigments. Maybe a rare tumor type. What about a solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas? Those can have cystic components and occur in young women. But the histo doesn't quite match. Alternatively, an adrenal tumor, but hormones are normal. The pigments might be key. Melanoma was ruled out. Could it be a hepatocellular carcinoma? But the location is near pancreas, and markers are negative. 

Wait, golden-brown pigment in the tumor cells... think about oncocytoma. Those have granular eosinophilic cytoplasm due to mitochondria, but here it's clear cytoplasm. Hmm. Another thought: clear cell tumors. Maybe a clear cell variant of something. The negative melanoma markers rule out clear cell sarcoma. The pigments might be bile or something else. The cyst adhered to pancreas head—could it be a pancreatic cystadenoma? Serous or mucinous. Serous has clear cells, but usually has glycogen, not pigments. Mucinous might have different cells. 

Wait, the histopathology mentions golden-brown pigments. That's characteristic of what? Maybe a pheochromocytoma, but those are adrenal and usually have different features. Or maybe a melanocytic tumor, but that's ruled out. Alternatively, a tumor with hemosiderin deposits, like a hemangioma? But the cells are described as large and polygonal. Another possibility: a lipid-rich tumor, but the cytoplasm is clear. The combination of clear cytoplasm and pigment is tricky. 

Wait, in the liver, there's a tumor called eosinophilic solid and cystic (ESC) carcinoma, which has cells with granular cytoplasm, but maybe not. Alternatively, a perivascular epithelioid cell tumor (PEComa), which can have clear cells and melanin-like pigment, but IHC markers would help. However, the case states that melanoma and metastatic markers are negative. PEComas can express HMB-45 or Melan-A, but if those are negative, then maybe not. 

Another angle: the location near the pancreas and the cyst. Solid pseudopapillary tumors can have cystic areas and occur in young women. Histologically, they have cells with clear or eosinophilic cytoplasm and may have hemorrhagic areas. The pigments could be from hemorrhage, like hemosiderin. The CT shows a hyper-dense cyst, which might be hemorrhagic. The low albumin and prolonged PT could be due to liver involvement or malnutrition from a chronic process. 

But the key here is the histopathology. The cells are large, polygonal, with golden-brown pigments and clear cytoplasm. Negative for melanoma markers. Let's think of adrenal cortical carcinoma, but adrenal hormones are normal. Wait, the CT mentions the cyst is adhered to the pancreas head, so maybe a pancreatic tumor. Alternatively, a retroperitoneal tumor. 

Wait, another possibility: a neuroendocrine tumor with pigmentation? Unlikely. Or a rare tumor like a paraganglioma, but location and IHC would differ. 

Wait, golden-brown pigment in the cytoplasm... Could it be lipochrome pigment, which is seen in some adrenal tumors or hepatocellular lesions? But the markers are negative for metastasis, so primary tumor. 

The combination of clear cytoplasm and pigment makes me think of a clear cell variant with hemosiderin or other pigment accumulation. Maybe a renal cell carcinoma (RCC) metastatic to the abdomen, but the patient has no mentioned history, and markers are negative. 

Wait, the patient is a young female. Another thought: a Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor, but those are ovarian. However, the personal history says no gynecological issues. The mass is in the right hypochondrium, so maybe not ovarian. 

Alternatively, a gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST), but those usually are in the stomach or small intestine. The CT shows adhesion to pancreas, maybe a pancreatic origin. 

Wait, the histopathology report says tumor cells are negative for melanoma and metastatic markers. So primary tumor. The clear cytoplasm and pigment... Maybe an epithelioid angiomyolipoma (a type of PEComa), but again, markers would be needed. 

Hmm, maybe the key is the golden-brown pigments. In the liver, there's focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH) with central scar, but that's a different presentation. 

Wait, the patient had a cyst. A cystic neoplasm in the pancreas. Serous cystadenoma is usually microcystic and has clear cells with glycogen. Mucinous cystic neoplasms have columnar cells. Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) is another possibility. But the histo here shows polygonal cells with pigments. 

Another angle: the prolonged PT and low albumin suggest possible liver dysfunction, but the adrenal hormones are normal. Maybe the tumor is causing some compression or paraneoplastic syndrome. 

Putting it all together: the mass is in the right hypochondrium, adhered to the pancreas head. The histology shows large polygonal cells with golden-brown pigments and clear cytoplasm. Negative for melanoma and metastatic markers. 

Wait, golden-brown pigment in the cytoplasm... Could this be a sign of oncocytic cells? But oncocytomas have granular cytoplasm due to mitochondria. Alternatively, a tumor with abundant lysosomes or lipofuscin. Lipofuscin is a yellowish-brown pigment associated with aging or degenerative changes. 

Another thought: the pigments could be bile, suggesting a hepatobiliary origin. But the CT showed a cyst adhered to the pancreas, not the liver. 

Wait, the CT mentions a hyper-dense cyst. Hyperdense on CT could indicate proteinaceous content or hemorrhage. If the cyst is hemorrhagic, the pigment could be hemosiderin, which is golden-brown. So the tumor might have areas of hemorrhage, leading to hemosiderin-laden macrophages. But the histopathology describes tumor cells themselves having the pigment. 

In that case, maybe a tumor that produces pigment, like a melanoma, but that's ruled out. Alternatively, a tumor with intrinsic pigment production. 

Wait, there's a rare tumor called pigmented paraganglioma, which can have melanin-like pigment, but those are usually in the adrenal medulla or paraganglia. Another rare tumor is melanotic schwannoma, but again, melanoma markers might be positive. 

Alternatively, a clear cell sarcoma, which can have melanin-like pigment, but the case states that melanoma markers are negative. Clear cell sarcoma is usually positive for S-100 and SOX10, but if those are not checked, maybe. However, the answer may not be that. 

Alternatively, considering the location near the pancreas and the histology, maybe a solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (SPN). SPNs have cells with eosinophilic or clear cytoplasm, and may have hemorrhagic cysts. The histopathology might show foamy cells or hemosiderin. The age and sex fit, as SPN is more common in young women. The tumor is solid and cystic, which aligns with SPN. However, the presence of golden-brown pigments isn't a classic feature, but could be due to old hemorrhage. 

But the key is the immunohistochemistry. SPN typically expresses beta-catenin (nuclear), CD10, and vimentin. If those markers were checked, it would help, but the case doesn't mention them. However, since the IHC ruled out melanoma and metastasis, perhaps SPN is a possibility. 

Another possibility: acinar cell carcinoma of the pancreas, which can have solid and cystic patterns, but usually presents in older adults. 

Alternatively, an adrenal cortical carcinoma, but the adrenal hormones are normal, making this less likely. 

Wait, the histopathology says the tumor cells have clear cytoplasm. Clear cell tumors in the pancreas are rare but possible. Metastatic renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is a consideration, but the patient is young and markers are negative. 

Alternatively, a PEComa (perivascular epithelioid cell tumor), which can occur in various locations, including the pancreas. PEComas can have clear to eosinophilic cytoplasm and may express melanocytic markers like HMB-45 or Melan-A, but if those are negative, maybe not. 

Alternatively, considering the location adhered to the pancreas and the cystic nature, maybe a mucinous cystic neoplasm (MCN) of the pancreas, which typically occurs in women, but the histology here doesn't mention mucin. 

Wait, the patient's symptoms: acute abdomen with fever, high CRP. Could this be an infected cyst or abscess? But the histopathology points to a tumor. 

Putting it all together, the most plausible diagnosis given the histology (large polygonal cells with golden-brown pigments and clear cytoplasm) and location (pancreas head) in a young female is solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas. However, the presence of pigments isn't classic. Alternatively, considering the clear cells and pigments, maybe a PEComa, but without markers. Another option is an adrenal tumor, but hormones are normal. 

Wait, another rare tumor: a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with clear cells and pigmentation? Unlikely. 

The key here is the combination of the histopathological findings. Large polygonal cells with clear cytoplasm and golden-brown pigments. If metastatic markers are negative, it's a primary tumor. The pigments could be lipofuscin or hemosiderin. 

In the pancreas, solid pseudopapillary neoplasms can have variable histology, including clear cells and foamy macrophages with hemosiderin. The presence of a cystic structure adhered to the pancreas, in a young female, with these histological features, makes SPN the most likely diagnosis. 

Alternatively, if the tumor is in the adrenal gland, but the CT says it's adhered to the pancreas head. Adrenal is more posterior. The CT mentions the cyst is in the right hypochondrium, which could be near the pancreas. 

Another thought: the tumor could be a mesenteric cyst, but the histology doesn't support that. 

In conclusion, considering all the factors—age, sex, clinical presentation, imaging, and histopathology—the most likely diagnosis is solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas. The golden-brown pigments might be due to hemosiderin deposits from previous hemorrhage within the tumor. The other possibilities don't fit as well. The negative melanoma markers and metastatic markers suggest it's a primary pancreatic tumor, and SPN is a common one in this demographic.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s acute presentation with right iliac pain, nausea, vomiting, and fever initially suggests appendicitis (supported by positive McBurney’s and Rovsing’s signs). However, the presence of a tender mass in the right hypochondrium and positive Murphy’s sign (though gallbladder ultrasound was normal) complicates the picture, pointing to alternative abdominal pathology.  
<step 2> Lab findings (elevated CRP, neutrophilia) confirm inflammation or infection. Low albumin and prolonged PT suggest possible liver dysfunction or malnutrition, but normal adrenal hormones exclude adrenal insufficiency. The hemoglobin drop (improved post-transfusion) hints at acute bleeding, likely related to the mass.  
<step 3> Imaging reveals a large cystic mass adherent to the pancreatic head. The hyper-dense cyst on CT and its adherence to the pancreas localize the lesion to the pancreatic region, narrowing differentials to pancreatic or peri-pancreatic tumors.  
<step 4> Histopathology shows tumor cells with golden-brown pigments, clear cytoplasm, and negative melanoma/metastatic markers. Golden-brown pigments (likely hemosiderin or lipofuscin) and clear cytoplasm suggest a primary neoplasm with degenerative or hemorrhagic changes. The absence of melanocytic/markers excludes melanoma or metastatic lesions.  
<step 5> The patient’s age, sex, and imaging correlate with solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (SPN) of the pancreas, a tumor common in young women. SPN often presents as a cystic-solid mass with hemorrhagic degeneration, aligning with the cyst’s hyper-density and pigment-laden histology. Other possibilities (e.g., PEComa, neuroendocrine tumor) are less likely due to clinical and immunohistochemical findings.  

### Answer:
Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas
